The target's current proof (read it on disk, §"The acyclic-resolution comparison

staircase. Cut it into these leaves (names are suggestions — assign `\lean{}`

`rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic` formalizes only part (1). Split **part (2)** into
its OWN block with its OWN `\lean{}` name (suggest
`CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic` or similar): for a SES
`0 → A → J → Z → 0` with `J` right-`G`-acyclic,
`(R¹G)(A) ≅ coker(G(J) → G(Z))`. Proof: the low-degree segment of the
right-derived LES `G(J) → G(Z) →[δ⁰] (R¹G)(A) → (R¹G)(J)=0` is exact with
vanishing right end, so `δ⁰` is epi with kernel `im(G(J)→G(Z))`; hence `δ⁰`
identifies `coker(G(J)→G(Z)) ≅ (R¹G)(A)`. Build it as a SIBLING of the existing
`rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES` engine, reusing `homology_exact₂/₃` at
degrees 0,1 and `Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf` (`R⁰G ≅ G`). After splitting,

COVERAGE` once part (2) has its own home, or repoint it) and its `\uses` so the
staircase target `\uses` the new part-(2) block by label. Keep
`lem:acyclic_dimension_shift`'s statement of part (1) and its `\lean{}` unchanged.

**Staircase assembly (rewritten target proof).** Compose part (1) of the
dimension shift down the staircase `(RⁿG)(A) ≅ (Rⁿ⁻¹G)(Z¹) ≅ ⋯ ≅ (R¹G)(Zⁿ⁻¹)`

case is the `H⁰ ≅ G(A) = (R⁰G)(A)` base. The target keeps its statement and
`\lean{}` unchanged; its proof becomes "by `lem:cosyzygy_ses`,
`lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution`, `lem:acyclic_dimension_shift`(1), and the

2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition

10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}

126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127

157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when

165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.

173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted

175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.

179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}

233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}

270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
272	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_cocycle, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.twistPair}
273	  % NOTE (iter-005 review): \lean{} corrected. The monolithic name `ofShortExact_twist` was
274	  % never created; the prover (iter-005) realized this lemma's content as a cluster: the twist
275	  % family `horseshoeτ`, the cocycle identity `horseshoeτ_cocycle`, the recursion kernel
276	  % `twistPair`, and the augmentation `horseshoeβ` (with helpers horseshoeβ₁/horseshoeH/
277	  % horseshoeτZero/ιC0). Planner: consider splitting this block into per-declaration sub-blocks
278	  % for cleaner 1-to-1 coverage; see recommendations.md (session_5).
279	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
280	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
281	  \[
282	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
283	  \]
284	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
285	  identity
286	  \[
287	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
288	  \]
289	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
290	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
291	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives: the target
292	  summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism
293	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
294	\end{lemma}
295	
296	\begin{proof}
297	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
298	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
299	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
300	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
301	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
302	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
303	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
304	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
305	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
306	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
307	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
308	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
309	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
310	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
311	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
312	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
313	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
314	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
315	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
316	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
317	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
318	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
319	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
320	\end{proof}

358	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}

366	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
367	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
368	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
369	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
370	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible

\begin{lemma}[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}

    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma

  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
  % between abelian categories and assume
  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
  \]
  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
  \[
    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
  \]
  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
  initial segment
  \[
    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
  \]
  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing

  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism

\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}

The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
here.

\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
  % for $n \geq n_0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow A
      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
      \longrightarrow \cdots
  \]
  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
  \[
    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
  \]
  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
  \]
  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles}
  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
  \[
    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
  \]
  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
  \]
  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
  kernels under precomposition, so
  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso}
  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
  % for $n \geq n_0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
  \[
    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
    \qquad
    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
  \]
  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
  \[
    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
  \]
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
\end{proof}

\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
  resolution into the
  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
  \]
  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.

  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
  \[
    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
      = (R^0 G)(A),
  \]
  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
  \[
    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
  \]
  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).

  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
  \[
    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
  \]
  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
  isomorphisms down the staircase
  \[
    (R^n G)(A)
      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
      \;\cong\; \cdots
      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
  \]
  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
  \[
    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
  \]
  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
  \[
    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
  \]
  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).

  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.

  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
  This is the case \(n = 0\).

  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
  \[
    (R^n G)(A)
      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
      \;\cong\; \cdots
      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
  \]
  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
  acyclic:
  \[
    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
  \]
  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).

  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
\end{proof}
